did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used logistic regression and the Newton method for nevus detection and segmentation, along with OpenCV for image processing and contour detection, to analyze nevi in whole-face images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes a prospective clinical trial involving 600 patients to evaluate a new algorithm for nevus detection and segmentation. It includes specific sections for Methods (algorithm development, clinical trial design, statistical analysis) and Results (quantitative data on detection accuracy, Kappa values, p-values), indicating it presents original experimental research rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Automated Lesion Segmentation and Quantitative Analysis of Nevus in Whole-Face Images
brief_summary: This study developed an automated algorithm for detecting and segmenting facial nevi using whole-face images to address the limitations of dermoscopy-based screening. The method employed logistic regression and the Newton method for nevus detection, followed by OpenCV for edge segmentation and area measurement. A multicenter prospective clinical trial was conducted with 600 patients across five centers in Shanghai to evaluate the algorithm's effectiveness. The algorithm detected 2,672 nevi, achieving a Kappa value of 0.860 compared to diagnoses by dermatologists, indicating high agreement. Statistical analysis showed no significant difference in area measurements between the algorithm and doctors (P = 0.265). The results demonstrate that the algorithm is effective for automated facial nevus scanning and can serve as a preliminary screening tool.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study; the algorithm was developed to detect, segment, and measure facial nevi in whole-face images. The study included 600 patients with nevi, detecting 2,672 nevi in total.

Mentioned in the background and discussion as a serious disease related to nevus deterioration and for which early diagnosis is critical, but it was not a target class for the algorithm or the clinical trial.

The study notes that malignant transformation of a benign nevus is rare, implying the nevi studied were predominantly benign, though specific histological confirmation counts are not provided in the text.

all_task_types.details: The algorithm performs lesion detection and edge segmentation to identify abnormal skin regions and compute the area of nevi in whole-face images. The method uses logistic regression for pixel classification (normal skin vs. nevus) to generate a binary mask, followed by contour detection and area measurement.
The algorithm detects the presence of nevi on the face, reporting the number of detected nevi (2672) and comparing detection agreement with dermatologists using Cohen's kappa.

datasets_used.name: Multicenter Prospective Clinical Trial Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective clinical trial dataset collected from 5 medical centers in Shanghai, China (Shanghai Ninth People’s Hospital, Xin Hua Hospital, Shanghai Sixth People’s Hospital, Hua Shan Hospital, and Tong Ji Hospital) between February 2018 and June 2018. The dataset consists of whole-face images of patients with facial nevi, along with manual diagnoses and area measurements provided by dermatologists and plastic surgeons.

use.training: The algorithm was developed using logistic regression and the Newton method; specific training split counts are not reported, but the method relies on pixel-level RGB classification rather than a separate training set.
use.testing: The 600 whole-face images were used to evaluate the effectiveness of the algorithm by comparing its detection and segmentation results against the gold standard provided by 5 doctor groups (13 dermatologists and 2 plastic surgeons).
use.other: The dataset served as the basis for a controlled clinical trial to assess the algorithm's accuracy in nevus detection, edge segmentation, and area measurement.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare nevus detection results between the algorithm and the doctor groups to assess agreement.
To evaluate the consistency of area measurements between the algorithm and the doctor groups.
To define statistical significance for the paired t-test comparing area measurements.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in the area measurements of nevi between the algorithm and the doctor groups.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the area measurements of nevi between the algorithm and the doctor groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paired t-test showed no significant difference between the two groups' area results (P = 0.265, P > 0.05), indicating high consistency in area measurement.

Doctor Group (Gold Standard).Composition: 13 dermatologists and 2 plastic surgeons across 5 centers

Multicenter Prospective Clinical Trial C.notes: The algorithm detected 2672 nevi compared to 2557 nevi identified by the doctor group. The kappa value of 0.860 indicates high agreement between the algorithm's detection and the doctors' diagnosis.

Doctor Group.Measurement method: Manual marking by 3 dermatologists, averaged, calculated using Image-J

Multicenter Prospective Clinical Trial C.notes: For the 2362 nevi successfully detected by the algorithm, the paired t-test showed no statistically significant difference between the algorithm's area measurements and the doctors' manual measurements (P = 0.265, P > 0.05).

architecture_used.main_model: Logistic Regression with Newton Method for pixel classification, combined with OpenCV for image processing (Gaussian filtering, Otsu's thresholding, morphological transformations, and contour detection)

architecture_details.classification_logic: Pixel-wise binary classification of normal skin (flag 1) vs. nevus (flag 0) based on RGB values using the formula Y = 1 / (1 + e^-(W^T X + b)). Weights (W) and bias (b) were solved using the Newton method via maximum likelihood estimation.
preprocessing: Gaussian filter for denoising (convolution with 2D Gaussian kernel)
Binarization using Otsu's algorithm to automatically calculate threshold
post_processing: Morphological transformations (dilation and erosion) to remove noise and connect adjacent elements
Contour search on binarized image to identify lesion edges
Area calculation based on pixel count within contours

training_details.optimizer: Newton method (for solving the logistic regression weights)
training_details.loss_function: Log-likelihood function derived from maximum likelihood estimation
training_details.hyperparameters: Standard deviation (s) for Gaussian kernel (formula provided but specific value not reported in text)
training_details.training_schedule: Not applicable (algorithm relies on pixel-level RGB classification rather than iterative deep learning training; weights derived via Newton method optimization)

classification_or_output_head.output: Binary map of normal skin (1) and nevus (0), followed by contour extraction for area measurement

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian filtering (denoising) using a 2D Gaussian kernel with standard deviation s
Binarization using Otsu's algorithm to automatically calculate threshold
Morphological transformations (dilation and erosion) to remove noise and connect adjacent elements
Contour search on binarized image to identify lesion edges
conclusions: The automated algorithm demonstrated high agreement with dermatologists' diagnoses for nevus detection, achieving a Kappa value of 0.860.
The algorithm is effective for nevus detection, edge segmentation, and area measurement in whole-face images.
There was no statistically significant difference between the algorithm's area measurements and those of dermatologists (P = 0.265), indicating high consistency in quantitative analysis.
The algorithm has great effectiveness in automated facial nevus scanning and can serve as a preliminary screening tool.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding border detection, specifically noting that missed diagnoses often occurred near the lip, nostril, eyebrow, and hairline.
important_remarks: The study population was heavily female-biased (68.67% women, 31.33% men), which may limit generalizability to male populations.
The algorithm relies on traditional image processing (logistic regression, OpenCV) rather than deep learning, meaning performance improvement is limited by parameter adjustment and experience.
Missed diagnoses were concentrated in specific anatomical regions (lip, nostril, eyebrow, hairline), indicating a specific limitation in border detection for these areas.
The dataset was collected exclusively from five medical centers in Shanghai, China, introducing potential geographic and demographic bias.

The study did not report a separate training/validation split; the algorithm's weights were derived via the Newton method on pixel-level RGB classification, and the 600-patient cohort was used for evaluation against a gold standard.
The algorithm detected more nevi (2672) than the doctor group (2557), resulting in a high number of misdiagnoses (310 patients), suggesting potential over-segmentation or false positives.

The study acknowledges that while the algorithm is effective for screening, it does not replace the need for professional dermatological diagnosis, particularly for differential diagnosis of melanoma.
